Reference Object  ·  Catalogue No. RO-BRONZE-040  ·  Bronze / Buddhist Ritual Metalwork

Buddhist Temple Bell with Mantra Inscription

Cast relief inscription of the six-syllable mantra 唵嘛呢叭咪吽 (Oṃ Maṇi Padme Hūṃ) in Chinese transliteration — lotus-petal skirt, scroll frieze

Attribution: Chinese Buddhist temple bell, Ming–Qing tradition (broad range)
Region: China
Material: cast copper alloy — composition pending laboratory analysis
Fig. 1 — Full view, front face, showing two cartouches of the mantra inscription.
Fig. 2 — Three-quarter view, opposite face.
Fig. 3 — Detail, two cast characters (呢 / 叭) in recessed cartouches.
Fig. 4 — Detail of encrustation and fine crack adjacent to one cartouche.
Fig. 5 — Close-up, scroll frieze band and lotus-petal rim.
Fig. 6 — Close-up, character cartouche with crystalline surface deposit.

01 Object Description and Attribution

The object is a small-to-medium cast bell of the standard Chinese temple/hand-bell type: a domed crown with an integral suspension loop, a cylindrical waist encircled by cast relief cartouches, and a flared, wave-scalloped (lotus-petal) skirt bordered above by a continuous floral scroll frieze. The suspension cord and tassel visible in the photographs are a later, non-original fitting and are excluded from the diagnostic assessment below.

The waist carries the six-syllable mantra Oṃ Maṇi Padme Hūṃ in its standard Chinese phonetic transliteration, 唵嘛呢叭咪吽, distributed across the cartouches around the circumference (Figs. 1–3, 6). This transliteration is a well-documented feature of Chinese Buddhist material culture attested from the Ming dynasty onward, recorded in Chinese numismatic and amulet scholarship on Ming-period Buddhist charms bearing the same six characters. It reflects standard Han Buddhist devotional practice rather than a marker specific to any single monastic school or region, and its presence alone does not establish a Tibetan find-site or commission history for this particular bell.

Alloy composition — pending. No SEM–EDX or XRF data have yet been obtained for this object. The proposed Ming–Qing range given above is based on typology, epigraphy, and casting technique only. As demonstrated on companion pieces in this atlas, alloy composition can substantially narrow or revise a period attribution based on style alone; a firm period assignment for this bell is deferred until laboratory analysis is available and will be added to this page at that time.

02 GOM Diagnostic Analysis

2.1 Inscription and casting technique

The characters are not hand-engraved but cast in relief within shallow rectangular cartouches, with negative space cut through between strokes in several characters (openwork, visible in Figs. 3 and 6). This is consistent with a template-based mould-casting process — the character block was built into the mould pattern before pouring — and points to production in an organised foundry workshop rather than individual hand-finishing. The floral scroll frieze along the lower skirt is evenly spaced and of consistent depth around the full circumference, again indicating a competently prepared master mould rather than freehand incising after casting.

2.2 Patina

The dominant surface condition is a smooth, dark brown-to-black patina typical of long-term handling and atmospheric exposure. Concentrated around one cartouche (Figs. 4, 6) and along parts of the lower rim, there are pale, crystalline surface deposits with a rougher, more granular texture than the surrounding patina. Two readings of this deposit are possible from photography alone: a benign mineral or salt efflorescence consistent with dry storage conditions, or an active corrosion process (copper-chloride “bronze disease”), which is chemically unstable and would warrant conservation attention if confirmed. This cannot be resolved from images and should be checked directly.

2.3 Condition

A fine crack is visible running from the edge of one cartouche downward toward the waist moulding (Fig. 4). Its extent cannot be judged from the surface photograph alone.

2.4 Acoustic response

Per the author’s own examination, the bell produces a strong, clear, sustained tone when struck, despite the surface crack noted in 2.3. A through-wall crack in a cast bronze/brass bell typically produces a dull, damped, or double tone; a clear sustained resonance is a meaningful non-destructive indicator that the crack is superficial — confined to the patina/corrosion layer — rather than penetrating the full wall thickness. This is a genuinely useful field observation and is recorded here as such.

Inscription

Mould-cast relief characters in cartouches, not hand-engraved — organised workshop production.

Ornament

Even, continuous scroll frieze around full circumference — competent master mould.

Patina

Dark brown-black overall; localized pale crystalline deposit of undetermined nature near one cartouche.

Acoustic

Strong, clear, sustained resonance — indicates the visible crack is surface-level only.

Diagnostic markerObservationStatus
Alloy compositionNot analysedPending laboratory visit
Casting methodMould-cast relief inscription and frieze, openwork in some charactersObserved
PatinaDark brown-black, with localized pale crystalline depositObserved; nature of deposit unresolved
Structural conditionFine crack adjacent to one cartoucheObserved; extent unresolved
Acoustic resonanceStrong, clear, sustained toneObserved by the author; supports surface-only crack

03 Cultural Context

The Chinese phonetic rendering of Oṃ Maṇi Padme Hūṃ as 唵嘛呢叭咪吽 is attested in Chinese Buddhist devotional and numismatic material from the Ming dynasty onward and remained in common use through the Qing period, appearing on temple items, charms, and ritual metalwork across Han Buddhist practice generally, including in regions with strong Sino-Tibetan Buddhist exchange. Its presence on this bell places the object within that broad devotional tradition; it does not, by itself, identify a specific monastery, region, or find-history, and no such attribution is made here. The bell form itself — domed crown, cartouche band, lotus-petal skirt — is a standard type within Chinese temple metalwork and is likewise not diagnostic of a single region.
